skill.name: sympy
skill.description: Guides symbolic mathematics work with SymPy in Python, including exact algebra, calculus, equation solving, matrices, physics models, and mathematical code generation.
skill.summary: Solve and transform exact symbolic math problems with SymPy guidance.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, script, filesystem, and network indicators, but review found most are Markdown examples or mathematical terminology rather than executable skill behavior. The skill is publishable with warnings because it documents eval-capable representations, parsing user input, generated file writes, pickle loading, and optional promotion of an external K-Dense service.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Expression Reconstruction and Parsing Patterns
medium_findings.description: The reference material notes that SymPy string representations can be recreated with eval and shows interactive input passed to parse_expr. These are documentation examples, not hidden execution logic, but users could copy them into workflows that evaluate untrusted mathematical input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky patterns are directly documented and the same file includes a sanitization warning. Confidence is high for copy-paste risk, but there is no evidence of malicious execution by the skill itself.

medium_findings.title: Local File Generation and Pickle Loading Examples
medium_findings.description: The code-generation guide shows writing TeX, text, Python, LaTeX document, C source, and pickle files, and loading a pickle file. This is expected for a code-generation skill, but file writes and pickle loading require user review when adapted to real projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit in documentation examples and align with legitimate export workflows. Risk depends on whether users run adapted examples against untrusted paths or pickle files.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Hundreds of backtick and keyword alerts occur inside Markdown formatting or mathematical examples. Reviewed high-signal examples include SymPy Function construction, eigenvalue dictionary keys, ODE constants named C1 and C2, and mechanics terms; no external command execution or weak cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review shows these alerts are caused by Markdown inline code or domain vocabulary. No evidence was found of shell execution, credential access, or cryptographic code in these locations.

low_findings.title: External Resource Links and Product Promotion
low_findings.description: The skill links to official SymPy documentation and GitHub examples, and it asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. The links are visible and not exfiltration, but marketplace reviewers may want to assess the external-service promotion policy.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external links and promotional instruction are explicit in SKILL.md. They do not perform network requests, so the security risk is low and primarily policy-related.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented eval-Capable Representation
dangerous_patterns.description: A reference note states that srepr output can be evaluated to recreate an expression. Treat this as unsafe for untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The note directly references eval behavior, but it is explanatory documentation rather than executable skill code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive Parsing of User Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide shows input passed to parse_expr and later warns users to validate and sanitize parsed input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is concrete and security-relevant, but the documentation also includes a matching mitigation note.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pickle Serialization Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes saving and loading a pickle file. Loading pickle data from untrusted sources can execute arbitrary code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pickle example is directly present. It is a common legitimate pattern but unsafe when copied for untrusted files.

content.value_statement: Exact symbolic mathematics can be hard to structure correctly in Python. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused SymPy guidance for algebra, calculus, matrices, physics, and code generation.
actual_capabilities: Creates SymPy symbols, assumptions, expressions, and exact simplification workflows.
Guides derivatives, integrals, limits, series expansions, and differential equations.
Solves algebraic equations, systems of equations, and common symbolic transformation tasks.
Works through symbolic matrices, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, determinants, and linear systems.
Applies SymPy physics modules for mechanics, vectors, quantum examples, optics, and units.
Generates LaTeX, string output, Python functions, and compiled-language code from expressions.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not install or run SymPy by itself.
Some equations have no closed-form solution and may require numerical methods.
Generated code and parsed expressions need validation before use with untrusted input.
Advanced physics examples may require domain knowledge to verify assumptions and units.

use_cases.title: Derive Exact Calculus Results
use_cases.description: Use SymPy to compute derivatives, integrals, limits, and series while keeping symbolic parameters intact.

use_cases.title: Prototype Scientific Formulas
use_cases.description: Transform formulas, simplify equations, and generate numerical functions for simulation or analysis workflows.

use_cases.title: Build Math-Aware Python Tools
use_cases.description: Create expression parsers, output formatters, LaTeX exports, and generated code for symbolic math applications.

prompt_templates.title: Simplify an Expression
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the SymPy skill to simplify this expression exactly: [expression]. Explain each transformation in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Solve Equations Symbolically
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to solve this equation or system: [equations]. State assumptions, exact solutions, and any limitations.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Matrix Problem
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to compute the determinant, inverse, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors for this matrix: [matrix]. Interpret the result.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Verified Math Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to derive [formula], simplify it, generate executable Python or C code, and list validation tests before use.

output_examples.input: Simplify a trigonometric expression and explain why the result is exact.
output_examples.output: A concise derivation showing the identity used, the simplified symbolic result, and the assumptions needed for the variables.

output_examples.input: Solve a second-order differential equation with initial conditions.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step SymPy workflow that states the equation, applies conditions, returns the symbolic solution, and notes verification checks.

output_examples.input: Convert a symbolic formula into a numerical function for simulation.
output_examples.output: A generated-function plan with the simplified expression, input variables, expected units, and tests for representative values.

best_practices: Declare symbol assumptions early, such as real, positive, integer, or nonzero.
Validate generated formulas with substitution tests and known special cases.
Sanitize parsed input and avoid evaluating expressions from untrusted sources.
anti_patterns: Do not use floating-point approximations when exact symbolic results are required.
Do not assume simplify always finds the form you expect; try targeted transformations.
Do not load pickle files or parse expressions from untrusted users without controls.

faq.question: What is SymPy best for?
faq.answer: SymPy is best for exact symbolic mathematics, including algebra, calculus, equation solving, matrices, and formula transformation.

faq.question: Can this skill run calculations directly?
faq.answer: The skill provides guidance and examples. The user or agent still needs a Python environment with SymPy installed.

faq.question: Does it support numerical computation?
faq.answer: Yes, it covers lambdify, evalf, and code generation, but its main value is symbolic work before numerical execution.

faq.question: Can it solve every equation exactly?
faq.answer: No. Some equations have no closed-form solution, so numerical solvers or approximations may be required.

faq.question: Is expression parsing safe for user input?
faq.answer: Only with validation and restrictions. Avoid eval patterns and treat untrusted mathematical input as unsafe by default.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
